coredata.dc:title: Sonochemical preparation of polyaniline@TiO2 and polyaniline@SiO2 for the removal of anionic and cationic dyes
coredata.dc:creator: Maruthapandi, Moorthy
Eswaran, Lakshmanan
Luong, John H.T.
Gedanken, Aharon
coredata.dc:description: Polyaniline (PANI) synthesized by simple CDs (carbon dots) initiated polymerization has formed a composite with TiO2 and SiO2, respectively via a sonochemical method. These PANI@TiO2 and PANI@SiO2 composites were proven as effective adsorbent materials to rapidly adsorb the anionic and cationic dyes from wastewater at neutral pH and ambient temperature. Selected popular cationic and anionic organic dyes consisted of methylene blue (MB), brilliant blue (BB), Evans blue (EB), crystal violet (CV), Congo red (CR), rhodamine B (RB), and rhodamine 6G (R6G). The adsorption equilibria were governed by Langmuir and Freundlich isotherms. The kinetic results revealed that the PANI@TiO2 and PANI@SiO2 composite materials synthesized via the sonochemical method are efficient adsorbents compared to other adsorbent materials for the removal of organic dyes from the water. The adsorbed dyes were effectively desorbed from the composites, rendering the reusability of PANI@TiO2 and PANI@SiO2. The estimated adsorption capacities of PANI@TiO2 and PANI@SiO2 composites were 89, 93, 80, 94 and 74, 71, 61, 61 mg/g for MB, CR, CV, and R6G, respectively.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD-spectra of PANI, PANI@TiO2, and PANI@SiO2 (b) FTIR spectra of PANI, PANI@TiO2, and PANI@SiO2.
(a & b) SEM images of the PANI@TiO2 and PANI@SiO2, and c) SEM-EDX of the polymer composites.
(a) and (b) are the adsorption capacity of cationic and anionic dyes (100 mg/L) onto the PANI@TiO2 and PANI@SiO2 (50 mg).
(a) and (b) pseudo-first-order model of PANI@TiO2 and PANI@SiO2, (c) and (d) pseudo-second-order model of PANI@TiO2 and PANI@SiO2.
(a) Intraparticle diffusion model of PANI@TiO2 and (b) PANI@SiO2 intraparticle diffusion model of the adsorption of organic dyes onto PANI@SiO2.
(a), (b) (c) and (d) are the Langmuir, and (e), (f), (g) and (h) are the Freundlich isotherms for the adsorption of organic dyes onto PANI@TiO2 and PANI@SiO2.
Comparison of polyaniline composites made with various adsorbent materials and its adsorption equilibrium.

row.entry: MB, BB, CR, CV and R6G

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The adsorption kinetic models for organic dyes onto PANI@TiO2 and PANI@SiO2.
Intraparticle diffusion model for the organic dye adsorption on PANI@TiO2 and PANI@SiO2.

head.ce:title: Sonochemical preparation of polyaniline@TiO2 and polyaniline@SiO2 for the removal of anionic and cationic dyes

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Bar-Ilan Institute for Nanotechnology and Advanced Materials, Department of Chemistry, Bar-Ilan University, Ramat-Gan 52900, Israel

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Bar-Ilan Institute for Nanotechnology and Advanced Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Bar-Ilan Institute for Nanotechnology and Advanced Materials, Department of Chemistry, Bar-Ilan University, Ramat-Gan 52900, Israel

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Industrial Chemistry, Alagappa University, Karaikudi 630 003, Tamilnadu, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry, University College Cork, Ireland
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry, University College Cork, Ireland

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Polyaniline (PANI) synthesized by simple CDs (carbon dots) initiated polymerization has formed a composite with TiO2 and SiO2, respectively via a sonochemical method. These PANI@TiO2 and PANI@SiO2 composites were proven as effective adsorbent materials to rapidly adsorb the anionic and cationic dyes from wastewater at neutral pH and ambient temperature. Selected popular cationic and anionic organic dyes consisted of methylene blue (MB), brilliant blue (BB), Evans blue (EB), crystal violet (CV), Congo red (CR), rhodamine B (RB), and rhodamine 6G (R6G). The adsorption equilibria were governed by Langmuir and Freundlich isotherms. The kinetic results revealed that the PANI@TiO2 and PANI@SiO2 composite materials synthesized via the sonochemical method are efficient adsorbents compared to other adsorbent materials for the removal of organic dyes from the water. The adsorbed dyes were effectively desorbed from the composites, rendering the reusability of PANI@TiO2 and PANI@SiO2. The estimated adsorption capacities of PANI@TiO2 and PANI@SiO2 composites were 89, 93, 80, 94 and 74, 71, 61, 61 mg/g for MB, CR, CV, and R6G, respectively.

ce:section.ce:para: Similarly to polypyrrole and polythiophene, polyaniline (PANI) is a popular conducting polymer [1–4] with low toxicity and adjustable electrical conductivity [5,6]. This nitrogen-enriched conductive material also has other appealing features such as low-cost, high flexibility, ease of synthesis, and stability at ambient conditions [7–10]. The nanostructured conducting PANI has been synthesized by the chemical oxidative and electrochemically method [7,11–13]. Recently, our group has established a new polymerization route to prepare conductive polymers without a strong initiator [9]. Of note is the synthesis of PANI using carbon dots (CDs) as the only initiator [14–17]. In general, the application of PANI is currently limited, due to its poor mechanical properties. The PANI composites with different metal oxides [13,18–25] greatly improve their structural and physical properties for pertinent applications. TiO2@PANI, PANI/ZnO, PANI/ZrO2, PANI/SiO2, and PANI/Ag can be used for organic dye adsorption/removal [26–38]. However, the synthesis is lengthy and costly besides the low adsorption capacity and sluggish kinetics of the above composites. To date, the composites of PANI with titania and silica are prepared by γ- irradiation, inverted emulsion polymerization, and graft polymerization [37–39].

This paper focuses on the preparation of two PANI@TiO2 and PANI@SiO2 nanocomposites and their application for the removal of the selected dyes. The PANI synthesized using the carbon dots (CDs) as the only initiator is then decorated with TiO2 and SiO2 nanoparticles by ultrasonication. The composites are stabilized due to the formation of strong interaction between the polymer chain and the metal oxides. Consequently, there is a noticeable change in the backbone chain of the composites compared to the pristine PANI. To our knowledge, this is the first attempt for the preparation of the PANI@TiO2 and PANI@SiO2 by sonochemistry. Of only relevance is the synthesis of a stable polymer composite by incorporating CuO into polypyrrole by ultrasonication [16]. There are various chemical, biological and physical techniques that have been applied to remove organic dyes from water. However, these methods always show a few disadvantages such as time-consuming process, toxicity, energy-intensive, and high cost [32,40]. To our knowledge, the adsorption method is an effective, economic and easily operated process [41,42]. Several inorganic and organic adsorbent materials have been used to remove the dyes from the aqueous solution. However, such adsorbent materials are always suffering from problems in the separation, the formation of by-products and recovering of the adsorbent from wastewater [36,28]. The two composites are then characterized and proven as robust adsorbent materials for the rapid adsorption of selected cationic and anionic organic dyes through π-π stacking and electrostatic interaction.
Synthesis of CDs: All chemicals of analytical grade were purchased from Sigma–Aldrich Israel.

The CDs were prepared by a one-step sonochemical technique as described previously [9]. A glass beaker with 40 mL of polyethylene glycol-400 was placed in an oil bath at 60–70 °C. The ultrasonic transducer tip was dipped in the beaker and sonicated for 2 h with a 35% amplitude. At the end of sonication, a yellow color CD solution was observed.

Preparation of PANI: Aniline (1.0 g) was dissolved in 25 mL of 2 M HNO3 in a 100 mL beaker at room temperature, followed by the addition of 3 mL of an aqueous solution of CDs, containing 5 mg of CDs. The reaction mixture was illuminated with UV light for 48 h, resulting in a blackish-brown slurry. The blackish-brown solid was filtered, washed with distilled water and dried at room temperature.

Synthesis of PANI@TiO2/SiO2: 10 mL of deionized water with 0.1 g of Ti (IV) isopropoxid/tetraethyl orthosilicate was added to a solution of 90 mL of ethanol containing 0.25 g of PANI. The reaction solution was sonicated until the temperature reached 60 °C, followed by the addition of ammonia to attain pH 8–9 (~0.2 mL). The tinted solution turned blackish-brown, which was subject to 30 min of sonication in a sonicated cell placed in an ice bath. The ratio of PANI: TiO2 and SiO2 is 8:2 (wt.).

Dye adsorption: All the organic dyes (100 mg each), methylene blue (MB), brilliant blue (BB), Evans blue (EB), crystal violet (CV), Congo red (CR), rhodamine B (RB), and rhodamine 6G (R6G), were dissolved in 1000 mL of deionized water. The dye concentrations were measured by UV–visible spectroscopy (Cary 100 spectrophotometer, Varian, operated by Lab Sphere software). Each dye (100 mL) with varying concentrations was added to 50 mg of PANI@TiO2 and PANI@SiO2. The suspension solutions were magnetically stirred at 1600 rpm with different contact times at pH 6.7 and 28 °C. Both neutral pH and room temperature were advocated to avoid the use of acid/base and excessive energy for practical applications. The adsorption process was monitored by measuring the dye concentration in the solution after the adsorbent removal by a filter syringe. After the adsorption reached equilibrium, the spent adsorbent materials were submerged in EtOH at room temperature with mixing to dissociate the adsorbed dyes. The resulting aqueous mixture was then centrifuged for 5 min to recover the adsorbents from ethanol for reuse. After the completion of the adsorption process, the PANI@TiO2 and PANI@SiO2 were placed into deionized water with stirring for 30 min. No leaching of the dyes was observed, attesting the strong binding between the dyes and the composites.

The PANI materials were characterized by various analytical techniques and reported in our previous publication [9]. The PANI composites were investigated by FTIR using a Transon 27 spectrometer (Bruker, Germany) in the range of 400–4000 cm−1. The composite morphology was observed using high-resolution scanning electron microscopy (HRSEM) by an FEI Megallon 400 L microscope. The crystalline properties of the polymer composites were assessed using an X-ray diffraction technique measured by a Bruker (Germany) AXS D8 Advance diffractometer. The dye concentrations were analyzed by UV–visible spectroscopy (Cary 100 spectrophotometer, Varian, operated by Lab Sphere software).

The PANI shows a very broad XRD diffraction band with 2θ = 20–40°, owing to the periodicity in the polyaniline structure parallel to the polymer chains. The PANI@SiO2 exhibits a narrower peak of 2θ = 24°, indicating the formation of SiO2 on the polymer surface (Fig. 1 a). The PANI@SiO2 also displays a drastically reduced diffraction intensity compared to the PANI, possibly due to the SiO2 ensuing from the realization of a stable composite with the polymer chain. Similar behavior was also observed for PANI@TiO2 with the smallest intensity. Such results attested that the PANI and its related PANI@TiO2 and PANI@SiO2 composite materials were amorphous in nature. The XRD patterns of TiO2 and SiO2 were available from our previous reports [43–48].

The PANI shows an FTIR spectrum with a broadband ~1059–1724 cm−1, the broad peak at 3272 cm−1 is attributed to NH stretching, and the peak at 3098 is due to the CH aromatic stretching vibration [15]. The peaks at 1595 and 1516 cm−1 are ascribed to CC stretching vibration. The peak at 1331 cm−1 is due to the C-N stretching and the peaks at 1176 and 1252 cm−1 are owing to the CH in-plane vibrations (Fig. 1b) [14,9,17]. The FTIR spectra of PANI@TiO2 and PANI@SiO2 unravel that the amine stretching vibration of NH and CN stretching are extremely reduced sharp narrow peaks. The aromatic stretching vibration ~3098 cm−1 and 1252–1085 cm−1 (CH in-plane vibration) are entirely disappeared due to the strong formation of metal oxides onto the polymer [16]. These reduced intensities and disappearances of the peaks confirmed the formation of the polymer composites of TiO2 and SiO2 with PANI.

SEM images and SEM-EDS basic elemental mapping of the PANI@TiO2 and PANI@SiO2 polymer composites are shown in Fig. 2 . The PANI@TiO2 and PANI@SiO2 composites exhibit irregular morphology with an average size of 5 µm. The EDX spectra and SEM-EDX elemental mapping spectra of the polymer composites show the elementary components (C, N, O, and Ir) with uniform distribution. For SEM analysis. a small amount of the material was placed on a carbon tape attached to a copper strip, and the composites were coated with Ir to improve the conductivity. Fig. 2(a & b) also affirmed the formation of spherical shape particles of TiO2 and small flake shape particles of SiO2 with the polymer. The SEM-EDS elemental mapping spectra of the PANI@TiO2 and PANI@SiO2 further confirmed the presence of Ti and Si.

The organic dyes aqueous solution was prepared by dissolving the dyes in deionized water to make a series of fixed concentrations. The adsorption capacity of organic dyes onto PANI@TiO2 and PANI@SiO2 is illustrated in Fig. 3 . The adsorption capacity for organic dyes was calculated as follows:
(1)
q = (
C 0
- C ) / m
(2)
R =
100
C o
-
C t
C o
where q is the number of organic dyes adsorbed per unit mass of PANI@TiO2 and PANI@SiO2, m (g) is the adsorbed mass of PANI@TiO2 and PANI@SiO2, V(L) is the volume of the organic dyes, C o is the initial concentration of dyes and C f (mg/L) is the final concentrations after the adsorption process of the dyes. The organic dyes removal efficiency (R) was calculated by equation (2). Except for EB, the adsorption process reached equilibrium within 60–100 min.

The comparison of the adsorption equilibrium between other adsorbent materials was provided in Table 1 . However, these adsorbent materials were applicable only for selective dye and show an adsorption activity against these specific dyes. Nevertheless, the adsorbent of PANI@TiO2 demonstrated the efficient adsorption capacity for MB, CR, CV and R6G whereas PANI@SiO2 revealed the maximum adsorption capacity for MB, BB, CR, CV, and R6G.

The kinetic models of pseudo first order and pseudo second order are shown in Fig. 4 (a), (b). The linearized pseudo first and second order kinetics models are described by equations 3–4:
(3)
ln
q e
-
q t
= ln
q e
-
k 1
t
(4)
t
q t
=
1
k 2
q
e
2
+
t
q e
where q t and q e mg/g represents the number of organic dyes adsorbed at the equilibrium concentration and k 1 (min−1) is the rate constant of the pseudo-first-order model and k 2 (g mg−1 min−1) is the pseudo-second-order model rate constant. The kinetic parameters such as k 1, k 2, correlation coefficients, and calculated q e,cal values are resolved by a linear equation as shown in Table 2 . The q e,cal values of both models agree well with the experimental data.

The adsorption kinetic studies are also investigated by using the Weber and Morris intraparticle diffusion model. In brief, if intraparticle diffusion is the rate-controlling factor, the uptake of the adsorbate is proportional to the square root of adsorption time (equation (5)).
(5)
q t
=
k i
t
1 / 2
+ C

The slope signifies k i (mg g−1 min−1/2) or the diffusion constant and the intercept represents C (mg/g). The intraparticle kinetic model indicates two steps of diffusion on the adsorption process. The first stage of the diffusion model specifies the film diffusion model where the diffusion of organic dyes from the adsorption to the external surface of PANI@TiO2 and PANI@SiO2. The second step of the diffusion model is assigned to the intraparticle diffusion stage owing to the gradual adsorption on the surface of PANI@TiO2 and PANI@SiO2. As shown in Fig. 5 (a), and 5(b), the slope (k i) of the intraparticle diffusion stage is smaller than that of the film diffusion stage. This intraparticle kinetic model specifies that the adsorption process of the intraparticle diffusion stage is a gradual process. The high values of C reveal that the extra mass transfer of dye molecules onto PANI@TiO2 and PANI@SiO2 are important in the sorption process and arise in the initial adsorption. The intraparticle diffusion model values were summarized in Table 3 .

The kinetic models of the adsorption isotherms are examined to offer complete evidence of the surface adsorbent properties of the polymer composites and the nature of the adsorption process. The adsorption process reaches the equilibrium adsorption is initiated once the rate of dye adsorption is equal to the desorption rate. The adsorption data are well fitted to the Freundlich and Langmuir models (Fig. 6 ). In the Langmuir model, the adsorption arises on a monolayer in all adsorption sites as the adsorbent surface is homogeneous. The Freundlich adsorption assumes that the multilayer adsorption process is localized on a heterogeneous surface. The linear form equations of the two models are presented as follows:
(6)
C
e
q e
=
1
q 0
K L
+
1
q 0
C
e
(7)
l n
q e
= l n
K F
+
1
n
C e
where qo (mg/g) is adsorption capacity and KL (L/mg) is the Langmuir constants in adsorption rate. In the case of the Freundlich model, n and KF are the constants. The Langmuir and Freundlich isotherms of PANI@TiO2 and PANI@SiO2 are shown in Fig. 6. The values of KL, qo, KF, and n are determined from the isotherms and the values are shown in Table 2. The R 2 value of the Freundlich and Langmuir model is 0.8 to 1, respectively, validating their applicability. The monolayer adsorption value determined from the Langmuir isotherm for PANI@TiO2 and PANI@SiO2 is in agreement with the experimental data. These results from adsorption isotherms values indicate that the adsorption of organic dyes on to PANI@TiO2 and PANI@SiO2 obeys the isotherms models.

The adsorption feasibility is evaluated by the Langmuir isotherm separation factor (RL) as shown in equation (8).
(8)
R
L
=
1
1 + b
C 0
where KL (L/mg) is the Langmuir constant and Co (mg/L) is the initial organic dyes concentration. The values of RL signify the nature of the isotherm; 0 < RL < 1 (favorable), RL = 0 (irreversible) RL > 1 (unfavorable) and RL = 1 (linear). The estimated RL value of organic dyes adsorption process onto PANI@TiO2 and PANI@SiO2 is below 1. These RL values are signifying a favorable adsorption isotherm. The Freundlich isotherms KL values are higher than the Langmuir isotherm KF, indicating that the adsorption method conforms to the Freundlich isotherm model. When 1/n > 1, the absorption coefficient increases with increasing concentration to reveal the consequence of an increase in hydrophobic surface features after monolayer. Once 1/n < 1, KF decreases with concentration. 1/n is a function of the strength of the polymer composite material. The value of 1/n is ≥ 1, demonstrating the adsorption of the organic dyes on to PANI@TiO2 and PANI@SiO2 are a multilayer adsorption process and not involved in any bond formation with the polymer composite material. The adsorption process arises first on a monolayer and slowly becomes a multilayer process with increasing contact times and extents equilibrium. The adsorption of dyes is well signified by the Freundlich isotherm. The Freundlich and Langmuir isotherms of PANI@TiO2 and PANI@SiO2 are displayed in Fig. 6 (a-d) and Fig. 6 (e-h), respectively. The values of KL, qo, KF, and n are summarized in Table 4 .

The facile one-step sonochemical method of decorating polyaniline with TiO2 and SiO2 results in the polymer composites for the removal of the cationic and anionic organic dyes at low concentrations. The adsorption equilibrium performance can be defined as the Langmuir and Freundlich isotherms. The two adsorption stage steps that occur during the gradual adsorption process and are determined by Weber’s intraparticle diffusion models. Both the adsorption capacity and kinetics are compared favorably with the literature reports.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ultsonch.2019.104864.
